provisions.raw_text: Section 1860D–14(a) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–114(a)) is amended— (1) in paragraph (1)(D)(ii), by striking ''that does not exceed $1
notes: Restructures Part D copayment structure for low-income beneficiaries effective plan year 2028
Before 2028: $1 for generics/preferred multiple-source drugs, $3 for other drugs (or less if the standard copayment is lower)
For 2028 and after: $0 for generics; CPI-U indexed amounts for preferred multiple-source drugs and other drugs, rounded to nearest 5 or 10 cents respectively
Also amends paragraph (4)(A)(ii) to limit certain adjustments to years before 2028

provisions.description: Establishes the Requiring Enhanced and Accurate Lists of (REAL) Health Providers Act. For plan year 2028 and subsequent plan years, requires specified Medicare Advantage plans to maintain accurate publicly available provider directories, verify directory information at least every 90 days, remove delisted providers within 5 business days, and provide cost-sharing protections to enrollees who rely on inaccurate directory information. Also requires MA organizations to annually conduct and report provider directory accuracy analyses, and requires accuracy scores to be posted publicly starting 2029.
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
Section 1852(c) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–22(c)) is amended— (1) in paragraph (1)(C)— 
(A) by striking ''plan, and any'' and inserti
notes: Subsection (a) amends Section 1852(c) of SSA to add provider directory accuracy requirements
Subsection (b)(1) amends Section 1852(d) of SSA to add cost-sharing protections when enrollee relies on inaccurate directory
Subsection (b)(2)(A) amends Section 1857(e) of SSA to require annual accuracy analysis and reporting
Subsection (b)(2)(B) amends Section 1851(d)(4) of SSA to require accuracy score display in provider directory starting 2029
Applies to 'specified MA plans': network-based plans and PFFS plans meeting access standards through contracts
Cost sharing protection: enrollee pays lesser of in-network or out-of-network cost sharing when provider was incorrectly listed
MA organizations must notify enrollees of cost-sharing protections

provisions.program_name: Medicare coverage of multi-cancer early detection screening tests
provisions.statutory_reference: Section 1861 of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395x) and Section 1834 of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395m)
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 6221. 
Medicare coverage of multi-cancer early detection screening tests

(a) 
Coverage
Section 1861 of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395x) is amended
notes: Subsection (a) amends Section 1861 to define multi-cancer early detection screening tests (MCED) and add them as covered Part B benefit effective January 1, 2029

Subsection (b)(1) amends Section 1834 to establish payment amounts: before Jan 1, 2031, at the rate for multi-target stool DNA tests; on or after Jan 1, 2031, the lesser of that rate or the CLFS rate under Section 1834A
Coverage limited to individuals aged 50+, with an upper age limit starting at 65 in 2029 and increasing by 1 year each subsequent year; frequency limited to once every 12 months
Subsection (b)(2) makes conforming amendments to Sections 1833 and 1862 of the SSA
Creates new mandatory Medicare spending as a new Part B benefit category

provisions.program_name: Medicare coverage of external infusion pumps and non-self-administrable home infusion drugs
provisions.statutory_reference: Section 1861(n) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395x(n))
provisions.period: beginning with the first calendar quarter beginning on or after the date that is 1 year after the date of enactment
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 6222. 
Medicare coverage of external infusion pumps and non-self-administrable home infusion drugs

(a) 
In general
Section 1861(n) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395x(n)) is amended
notes: Subsection (a) amends the DME definition to treat external infusion pumps and associated home infusion drugs as meeting the 'appropriate for use in the home' requirement even if they normally require administration by a health care professional

Conditions: (1) FDA-approved prescribing information instructs administration by/under supervision of a health care professional; (2) a qualified home infusion therapy supplier administers/supervises in the home; (3) prescribing information instructs infusion at least 12 times per year intravenously, subcutaneously, or at rates requiring an external pump
Effective one year after enactment
Expands mandatory Medicare Part B spending by broadening DME coverage

cross_references.target: Section 1861(n) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395x(n))
cross_references.description: Adds new sentence expanding DME home-use requirement for external infusion pumps

In general
Section 1860D–4(b)(1) of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–104(b)(1)) is amended by striking subparagraph (A) and inserting the following:
notes: Subsection (a) of SEC. 6223; replaces current subparagraph (A) with any-willing-pharmacy requirement for Part D plans
Requires PDP sponsors to permit any pharmacy meeting standard contract terms and conditions to participate as network pharmacy

provisions.period: for plan years beginning on or after January 1, 2028
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
(b) 
Essential retail pharmacies
Section 1860D–42 of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–152) is amended by adding at the end the following new subsection:
notes: Subsection (b) of SEC. 6223; adds new subsection (e) to Section 1860D-42
Requires biennial reports (beginning 2028, at least once every 2 years until 2034, periodically thereafter) on essential retail pharmacies
Reports cover trends in reimbursement, dispensing fees, incentive payments, network participation, pharmacy counts, cost-sharing comparisons, dispensing volume comparisons
Defines essential retail pharmacy as non-affiliate retail pharmacy in rural (no other pharmacy within 10 miles), suburban (2 miles), or urban (1 mile) area
Requires CMS to publish list of essential retail pharmacies beginning plan year 2028
Requires PDP sponsors and MA organizations to submit affiliate pharmacy lists and incentive payment data
Secretary may implement by program instruction; exempt from Paperwork Reduction Act

In general

notes: Subsection (c) of SEC. 6223; adds new subparagraph (F) to Section 1860D-4(b)(1)
Establishes allegation submission process for pharmacies to report violations of reasonable and relevant contract standards by PDP sponsors, effective January 1, 2029

provisions.description: Amends Section 1860D-12 of the Social Security Act to add new subsection (h) imposing comprehensive requirements on pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) acting on behalf of PDP sponsors for plan years beginning on or after January 1, 2028, including: prohibition on income other than bona fide service fees, transparency regarding pricing guarantees, annual reporting requirements, audit rights for PDP sponsors, enforcement mechanisms including disgorgement, certification of compliance, anti-retaliation protections, and detailed definitions of affiliate, bona fide service fee, and pharmacy benefit manager.
provisions.policy_area: Medicare Part D pharmacy benefit manager regulation
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
Section 1860D–12 of the Social Security Act (42 U.S.C. 1395w–112) is amended by adding at the end the following new subsection: 

(h) 
Requirements relating to pharmacy benefit managers
notes: Subsection (a)(1) adds new subsection (h) to Section 1860D-12 of the Social Security Act
Effective for plan years beginning on or after January 1, 2028
Establishes detailed reporting requirements for PBMs including drug-level data, affiliate pharmacy information, generic vs. brand coverage justifications, and broker compensation disclosure
Requires PBMs to disgorge any remuneration received in violation of the bona fide service fee requirement
Establishes audit rights for PDP sponsors with no PBM-imposed limitations on auditor selection
Includes confidentiality protections for disclosed information with limited exceptions for Secretary, GAO, CBO, MedPAC, Attorney General, and OIG

SEC. 6227. 
Medicare sequestration
Section 251A(6) of the Balanced Budget and Emergency Deficit Control Act of 1985 (2 U.S.C. 901a(6)) is amended— (1)
notes: Subparagraph (D) is amended to set the Medicare payment reduction at 2.0 percent for its applicable fiscal year
New subparagraph (F) is added for FY2033: 2.0% reduction for the first 5 months, 0% reduction for the last 7 months
The 0% rate for the final 7 months of FY2033 effectively suspends Medicare sequestration for that period, notwithstanding the normal 2% limit in subparagraph (A)
This is a common pay-for mechanism used as a budget offset

notes: Subsection (b). provisions.description: Amends the Public Health Service Act (Title XXVII, Part D) and the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 to establish oversight and reporting requirements for entities providing pharmacy benefit management (PBM) services on behalf of group health plans and health insurance issuers. Requires PBM entities to submit semiannual (or quarterly upon request) reports to group health plans containing detailed information on drug costs, rebates, spending, formulary placement, affiliated pharmacy dispensing, and compensation to brokers/consultants. Requires plans to provide summary information and claims-level data to participants and beneficiaries upon request. Establishes civil monetary penalties of $10,000 per day for failure to report and up to $100,000 per item for knowingly providing false information. Directs the Secretary to promulgate regulations including a standard reporting format within 18 months of enactment. Includes privacy protections consistent with HIPAA and related statutes. Applies to plan years beginning 30 months or more after enactment for contracts entered into on or after that date.

provisions.description: Establishes comprehensive oversight and reporting requirements for entities providing pharmacy benefit management (PBM) services on behalf of group health plans and health insurance issuers. Adds new Section 726 to ERISA requiring PBM entities to report detailed drug cost, rebate, pricing, and spending information to group health plans at least semiannually. Requires contracts with applicable entities (manufacturers, wholesalers, GPOs, etc.) to include data-sharing provisions. Amends ERISA Section 502 to authorize the Secretary to impose penalties of $10,000 per day for nondisclosure violations and up to $100,000 per item of false information. Also amends the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, Chapter 100, with corresponding provisions.

SEC. 726. 
Oversight of entities that provide pharmacy benefit management services
(a) 
In general
For plan years beginning on or after the date that is 30 months af
notes: Adds new Section 726 to the Employee Retirement Income Security Act of 1974 (ERISA)
Effective date is plan years beginning 30 months after enactment
Requires PBM entities to submit semiannual (or quarterly upon plan request) reports with detailed drug pricing, rebate, and spending data to group health plans
Specified large employers (100+ employees) and specified large plans (100+ participants) subject to more granular per-drug, per-claim reporting under paragraph (2)(A)
All group health plans receive summary documents under paragraph (2)(B) regardless of size

Reports must include: contracted compensation to PBMs and pharmacies, net prices after rebates, total out-of-pocket spending, therapeutic class breakdowns, affiliated pharmacy disclosures, and broker/consultant compensation
Participants and beneficiaries may request summary documents and claim-level spread data
Privacy protections align with HIPAA and HITECH Act regulations; reports limited to summary health information
Enforcement via new ERISA Section 502(c)(13): $10,000/day for nondisclosure violations, up to $100,000 per item of false information
Secretary required to promulgate standard reporting format and implementing regulations within 18 months of enactment
Secretary to align reporting with existing Section 725 requirements
Secretary must define 'limited form' report for plans affiliated with drug supply chain participants to prevent anti-competitive behavior
Also amends ERISA Section 732(a) (29 U.S.C. 1191a(a)) to add Section 726 reference
Amends ERISA table of contents with clerical amendment
Amends Internal Revenue Code of 1986, Chapter 100, subchapter B (text appears truncated at end of provided excerpt)

provisions.description: Creates new Internal Revenue Code Section 9826 requiring entities providing pharmacy benefit management services on behalf of group health plans to submit semiannual (or quarterly upon request) reports to the plan containing detailed drug-level and therapeutic-class-level cost, rebate, dispensing, and formulary information. Prohibits plans or PBM entities from contracting with applicable entities that limit or delay disclosure of information necessary for reports. Requires plans to provide summary documents and claims-level information to participants and beneficiaries upon request. Establishes privacy requirements consistent with HIPAA, mandates standard reporting formats, and requires Secretary rulemaking within 18 months. Effective for plan years beginning 30 months after enactment.

provisions.description: Requires 100 percent pass-through of all rebates, fees, alternative discounts, and other remuneration received from applicable entities related to drug utilization or spending to the group health plan or health insurance issuer. Applies to contracts or arrangements for pharmacy benefit management services entered into or renewed on or after the date that is 30 months after enactment. Establishes quarterly remittance requirements, disclosure and audit rights, and rules for rebate aggregators.

provisions.description: Applies Section 7 of the Fair Labor Standards Act as if it includes a new subsection (s) exempting employees employed to adjust or evaluate claims resulting from a major disaster from overtime provisions for 2 years after the disaster. Applies to employees earning at least $591.00/week, employed by non-insurer employers, performing specified claims activities. Effective on date of enactment.
provisions.policy_area: Labor standards, overtime exemptions, disaster claims adjusters
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: (a) Section 7 of the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 (29 U.S.C. 207) shall be applied as if the following text is part of such section:
notes: Does not formally amend the FLSA; uses 'shall be applied as if' language
Exemption is for 2 years after a major disaster declaration
Minimum weekly compensation of $591.00 or any minimum established by the Secretary, whichever is greater
Applies only to employees of employers not engaged in underwriting, selling, or marketing insurance
Major disaster defined broadly as any disaster or catastrophe declared by any State or Federal agency or department
Subsection (b) makes section effective on date of enactment

cross_references.target: Section 7 of the Fair Labor Standards Act of 1938 (29 U.S.C. 207)
cross_references.description: Applied as if new subsection (s) were part of the section; functional amendment for overtime exemption of disaster claims adjusters

provisions.description: Allows employers in the seafood industry with an approved H-2B petition to bring nonimmigrants into the United States at any time during a 120-day period from the start date without filing another petition. After the 90th day, employers must complete a new local labor market assessment (listing job orders in newspapers and posting on DOL registry) and offer the job to qualified U.S. workers. Secretary of Labor shall not consider such employers to be staggering the date of need.
provisions.policy_area: Immigration, H-2B visa program, seafood industry
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text:
general
Subject to paragraph (2), if a petition for H–2B nonimmigrants filed by an employer in the seafood industry is granted, the employer may bring
notes: Provides 120-day entry flexibility for seafood industry H-2B workers
After 90 days, requires new labor market test: newspaper ads on 2 Sundays plus electronic job registry posting
Exempts seafood employers from anti-staggering rules at 20 CFR 655.20(d)
H-2B nonimmigrants defined as those admitted under INA section 101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B)

cross_references.target: Section 101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B) of the Immigration and Nationality Act (8 U.S.C. 1101(a)(15)(H)(ii)(B))

cross_references.description: Anti-staggering rule from which seafood employers are exempted

provisions.description: Establishes that the prevailing wage for the H-2B program shall be the greater of: (1) the actual wage paid by the employer to similarly situated employees, or (2) the prevailing wage level for the occupational classification in the geographic area. Requires the Secretary to accept private wage surveys even when Occupational Employment Statistics data are available, unless the survey methodology and data are not statistically supported.
provisions.policy_area: Immigration, H-2B visa program, prevailing wage
provisions.title: I
provisions.raw_text: The determination of prevailing wage for the purposes of the H–2B program shall be the greater of—(1) the actual wage level paid by the employer to other employees with similar experience and qualifications for such position in the same locati
notes: Mandates acceptance of private wage surveys for H-2B even when OES data exist
Only exception: Secretary determines private survey methodology/data are not statistically supported

provisions.description: Prohibits use of funds in this Act to enforce the definition of corresponding employment at 20 CFR 655.5 or the three-fourths guarantee rule at 20 CFR 655.20, or any references thereto. cross_references.description: DHS definition of temporary need substituted for H-2B purposes

provisions.description: Amends the Act creating the Department of Labor (37 Stat. 736) to authorize the Secretary of Labor to employ law enforcement officers or special agents for personal protection, protective intelligence work, coordination with local law enforcement, and related criminal investigations. Grants authority to carry firearms and make warrantless arrests. Requires compliance with Attorney General and Secretary guidelines.

provisions.description: Prohibits funds under the Refugee and Entrant Assistance heading from being used to house unaccompanied alien children in facilities not State-licensed for their care, except temporarily due to influx or emergency. Unlicensed facilities operating more than 6 months must comply with applicable Flores Settlement Agreement requirements, specified staffing ratios, and clinician ratios. The Secretary may grant up to four consecutive 60-day waivers with certification of good-faith compliance efforts. ORR must adhere to monitoring requirements from its May 15, 2019 Policies and Procedures Guide, conduct comprehensive monitoring visits for unlicensed facilities operating more than 3 months, and brief Congress within 60 days on influx facility requirements.

Paragraph (5): Quarterly monitoring visits for facilities operating 3+ months

provisions.description: Prohibits the use of any funds appropriated in this Act for any abortion or for health benefits coverage that includes coverage of abortion (Hyde Amendment).
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (a) None of the funds appropriated in this Act, and none of the funds in any trust fund to which funds are appropriated in this Act, shall be expended
notes: Subsection (a) prohibits funds for any abortion
Subsection (b) prohibits funds for health benefits coverage that includes abortion coverage
Subsection (c) defines 'health benefits coverage' as the package of services covered by a managed care provider/organization pursuant to a contract
Standard Hyde Amendment language applied to Labor-HHS-Education appropriations

provisions.description: Establishes exceptions to the abortion funding prohibition for cases of rape, incest, or life-endangering physical conditions. Clarifies that State/local/private funds (other than Medicaid matching) may be used for abortion. Preserves ability of managed care providers to offer abortion coverage with non-Federal funds.
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text:
(a) The limitations established in the preceding section shall not apply to an abortion— 
(1) if the pregnancy is the result of an act of rape or ince
notes: Subsection (a) provides exceptions for rape, incest, and life of the mother
Subsection (b) permits use of State/local/private funds (excluding Medicaid match) for abortions
Subsection (c) preserves managed care provider ability to offer abortion coverage with State funds (excluding Medicaid match)

provisions.description: Prohibits use of funds to discriminate against any institutional or individual health care entity on the basis that the entity does not provide, pay for, provide coverage of, or refer for abortions (conscience protection).
provisions.policy_area: Abortion conscience protection for health care entities
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (1) None of the funds made available in this Act may be made available to a Federal agency or program, or to a State or local government, if such agen
notes: Subsection (d)(1) prohibits funds for any entity that discriminates against health care entities refusing to provide/pay for/cover/refer for abortions

Subsection (d)(2) broadly defines 'health care entity' to include individual physicians, hospitals, provider-sponsored organizations, HMOs, health insurance plans, and any other health care facility, organization, or plan
This is the Weldon Amendment conscience protection language

provisions.description: Prohibits use of funds for the creation of human embryos for research purposes, or for research in which human embryos are destroyed, discarded, or knowingly subjected to risk of injury or death greater than that allowed for fetal research under 45 CFR 46.204(b) and section 498(b) of the Public Health Service Act (Dickey-Wicker Amendment).
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text:
(a) None of the funds made available in this Act may be used for— (1) the creation of a human embryo or embryos for research purposes; or 
(2) researc
notes: Subsection (a)(1) prohibits creation of human embryos for research
Subsection (a)(2) prohibits research destroying or endangering human embryos beyond what is allowed for fetal research
Subsection (b) defines 'human embryo or embryos' broadly to include organisms derived by fertilization, parthenogenesis, cloning, or any other means from human gametes or diploid cells
Standard Dickey-Wicker Amendment language

cross_references.description: Federal regulations on fetal research protections

provisions.description: Prohibits use of funds for any activity that promotes the legalization of any drug or substance in Schedule I of the Controlled Substances Act, except for normal executive-congressional communications. provisions.description: Prohibits reprogramming of funds without 15-day advance consultation and 10-day advance written notification to the Committees on Appropriations. Applies to current-year funds, prior-year carryover, and fee-derived funds. Covers creation of new programs, elimination of programs, increases to denied/restricted items, office relocations, reorganizations, and contracting out. Also restricts reprogramming exceeding $500,000 or 10 percent (whichever is less) that augments or reduces programs/activities.
provisions.title: V
provisions.raw_text: (a) None of the funds provided under this Act, or provided under previous appropriations Acts to the agencies funded by this Act that remain available
notes: Subsection (a) lists 7 categories of reprogramming that require advance consultation (15 days) and written notification (10 days): new programs, program elimination, increases to denied/restricted items, office relocation, office renaming, program reorganization, contracting out
Subsection (b) addresses reprogramming exceeding $500,000 or 10% (whichever is less) for augmenting existing programs, reducing programs by 10%, or general personnel savings affecting programs
Applies to funds from this Act, prior-year carryover, and fee-derived accounts
The $500,000 threshold is a de minimis limit for reprogramming notification, not new spending

provisions.description: Prohibits use of funds to require disclosure of political affiliation or voting history of candidates for Federal scientific advisory committees, or to require positions on political issues not directly related to the committee's work. notes: Transfer ceiling is percentage-based: not to exceed 10 percent of any covered appropriation, not a fixed dollar amount.

provisions.description: Requires that during FY2026, any Executive order or Presidential memorandum issued or revoked must be accompanied by a written OMB statement on budgetary impact, including costs, benefits, and revenues, with narrative summary, 5-year mandatory/discretionary impact by agency, and 5-year revenue impact. Emergency orders have a 15-day grace period. Threshold for Presidential memoranda is $100,000,000 in regulatory cost.
deadlines: 15 days after issuance for national emergency orders
provisions.title: II
provisions.raw_text:
SEC. 202. 
(a) During fiscal year 2026, any Executive order or Presidential memorandum issued or revoked by the President shall be accompanied by a written s
notes: Subsection (a) establishes the general requirement for OMB budgetary impact statements.
Subsection (b) specifies content: narrative summary, 5-year mandatory/discretionary impact by agency, 5-year revenue impact.
Subsection (c) provides 15-day grace period for national emergency orders/memoranda.
Subsection (d) limits applicability to Presidential memoranda with estimated regulatory cost exceeding $100,000,000.

provisions.description: Prohibits reprogramming of Federal and DC funds for agencies funded by this Act without prior approval from the Committees on Appropriations, if the reprogramming creates new programs, eliminates programs, changes specifically denied/limited allocations, increases funds or personnel for denied/restricted programs, re-establishes deferred programs, augments programs by more than $3,000,000 or 10 percent (whichever is less), or increases personnel by 20 percent or more. Authorizes DC to approve local fund reprogramming and transfer requests through November 7, 2026.

provisions.description: Amends the District of Columbia College Access Act of 1999 to increase the annual tuition payment cap from $10,000 to $15,000 and the aggregate cap from $50,000 to $75,000 (section 3), and the private institution annual cap from $2,500 to $3,750 and aggregate cap from $12,500 to $18,750 (section 5). Also adds a ratable reduction provision for students receiving more than $10,000 (section 3) or $2,500 (section 5) in an award year.

provisions.description: Prohibits U.S. assistance funds under titles III through VI from being provided under new bilateral agreements unless such agreements exempt U.S. assistance from taxation or provide for reimbursement. Establishes a penalty mechanism: 200% of taxes assessed during FY2026 on U.S. assistance by a foreign government shall be withheld from FY2027 and prior year obligations for that country if taxes are not reimbursed by September 30, 2027. Withheld funds shall be reprogrammed for non-taxing countries. Includes a de minimis exception and waiver authority for the Secretary of State. Requires the Secretary to issue rules/regulations/policy guidance to implement. Defines bilateral agreement and taxes/taxation.
provisions.title: VII

notes: Subsection (a): Core prohibition requiring tax exemption or reimbursement in new bilateral agreements.
Subsection (b): 200% penalty withholding for taxes assessed in FY2026, deadline September 30, 2027.
Subsection (c): De minimis exception for foreign taxes.
Subsection (d): Withheld funds reprogrammed to non-taxing countries.
Subsection (e): Secretary may waive if effective reimbursement arrangement exists or foreign policy interests outweigh; 15-day consultation required.
Subsection (f): Secretary shall issue implementing rules/regulations/guidance.
Subsection (g): Defines 'bilateral agreement' and 'taxes and taxation' (includes VAT and customs duties, excludes individual income taxes on local staff).

The prohibition terminates 12 months after the government ceases providing such equipment and applies to equipment provided under contracts entered into after October 1, 1997. The President may furnish restricted assistance upon determining it is important to the national interest, with reporting to the Committees on Appropriations.

provisions.description: Deems the period of the lapse in appropriations beginning on or about January 31, 2026, as covered by the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (P.L. 119-37), ensuring retroactive funding authority during the government shutdown.
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 102. For the purposes of the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (division A of Public Law 119–37), the time covered by such division shall be considered to include the period which began on or about January 31, 2026, during which there occurred a lapse in appropriations.
notes: Retroactively covers the government shutdown lapse period that began around January 31, 2026
Ensures CR funding authority applies to obligations incurred during the lapse

cross_references.target: P.L. 119-37, Division A
cross_references.description: Extends the time covered by the CR to include the lapse period

provisions.description: Makes amounts available under both the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (P.L. 119-37) and the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2026 for personnel pay, allowances, and benefits available for back-pay payments pursuant to 31 U.S.C. 1341(c), and directs that such payments shall be made.
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 103. Amounts made available in the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (division A of Public Law 119–37) and the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2026 for personnel pay, allowances, and benefits in each department and agency shall be available for payments pursuant to subsection (c) of section 1341 of title 31, United States Code and such payments shall be made.
notes: Requires back-pay for federal employees affected by the lapse in appropriations
References 31 U.S.C. 1341(c), the Antideficiency Act provision authorizing back-pay during funding gaps

cross_references.target: P.L. 119-37, Division A

provisions.description: Ratifies and approves all obligations incurred in anticipation of the appropriations made by the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (P.L. 119-37) and the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2026 for purposes of maintaining essential government activities, protecting life and property, and orderly termination of government functions during the lapse.
provisions.raw_text: SEC. 104. All obligations incurred and in anticipation of the appropriations made and authority granted by the Continuing Appropriations Act, 2026 (division A of Public Law 119–37) and by the Consolidated Appropriations Act, 2026 for the purposes of maintaining the essential level of activity to protect life and property and bringing about orderly termination of Government function, and for purposes as otherwise authorized by law, are hereby ratified and approved if otherwise in accord with the provisions of such Act.
notes: Standard shutdown ratification provision validating obligations incurred during the lapse period
Covers obligations for essential activities and orderly termination of government functions

cross_references.target: P.L. 119-37, Division A

notes: SEC. 6106(b)(1) sets general effective date: rate years beginning on or after enactment

notes: Subsection (f) of SEC. 6209
Extends telehealth hospice recertification encounters from January 30, 2026 to December 31, 2027

Adds new exception: telehealth encounters not permitted after January 31, 2026 if the beneficiary is in a hospice enrollment moratorium area, receiving care from a provider under enhanced oversight, or the encounter is by a non-enrolled or non-opt-out physician or practitioner
Also requires, for encounters on or after January 1, 2027, that claims include modifiers or codes indicating the encounter was via telehealth

Adds new paragraph (10) to Section 1834(m) of the Social Security Act

Applies to: (A) telehealth services furnished via a virtual platform by contracting physicians/practitioners or those with payment arrangements, and (B) telehealth services furnished incident to professional services
Creates new transparency requirements for telehealth billing
